label: chemrof:AnionState
description: When curating an Anion State, which serves as a mixin applied to any negatively charged atom or molecule, the data structure is flexible and remains open to additional properties beyond the core definition. Within this state, you may optionally record the elemental charge, which represents the number of protons minus the number of electrons. If this charge is specified, it must be provided as a single whole number. Furthermore, to accurately reflect the anionic nature of the entity, this integer must be negative one or lower.

label: chemrof:AtomGroupingByPeriodicTableBlock
description: When defining an Atom Grouping By Periodic Table Block, such as a group 13 atom, the data model is strictly closed, meaning no outside properties are permitted other than explicitly ignored system types. Every record must specify exactly one string-based identifier and may provide a single string representing the name of the chemical entity. The grouping can specify at most one periodic table block, which classifies elements unified by the orbitals of their valence electrons or vacancies, and this value must be chosen from a specific set of allowed CHEBI identifiers or the exact value 'g-block'. To establish hierarchical relationships, the record may include up to one OWL subclass categorization formatted as an internal nested record linking it to broader metaclasses. Furthermore, it can define subtype relationships using a direct link to a Molecule Grouping Class to indicate that this grouping is a more specialized form of another grouping class.

label: chemrof:AtomGroupingByPeriodicTableGroup
description: When defining an atom grouping by periodic table group, such as a p-block atom, the data model is strictly closed, meaning no outside properties are permitted other than explicitly ignored system types. Each record must specify exactly one string-based identifier. Additionally, the record may include a single optional string for the name of the chemical entity, such as nickel or carbon-16, as well as a single optional value indicating its specific periodic table group. To establish hierarchical categorizations, the grouping may contain a single internal nested record defining it as an OWL subclass. Finally, you may include direct links to one or more molecule grouping classes to establish a subtype relationship, indicating that this atom grouping is a more specialized form of those broader classes.

label: chemrof:AcidAnionGroupingClass
description: When defining an Acid Anion Grouping Class, which serves to group the different ion forms of an acid's base, the data model is strictly closed, meaning no outside properties are permitted other than explicitly ignored system types. Every record must specify exactly one string-based identifier. Additionally, it may include at most one string-based name for the chemical entity, provided that this name strictly ends with the suffix "ate". To define chemical relationships, the record can provide a direct link to a maximum of one chemical entity representing its conjugate acid, as well as a direct link to at most one chemical entity acting as its conjugate base, a relationship used to connect the base form or its various charge states, such as lactate, back to the parent acid. To support arbitrarily deep classification hierarchies, the entity may use direct links to categorize itself as a subtype of another molecule grouping class. Finally, the record allows for at most one internal nested record to establish a formal subclass relationship, categorizing the entity under a broader metaclass grouping.

label: chemrof:AcidBaseConflationClass
description: When defining an acid base conflation class, which is a molecule grouping class designed to group an acid together with all its physiological conjugate bases, the data model is strictly closed, meaning no outside properties are allowed except for the standard system type. Every record must specify exactly one string-based identifier. Additionally, the record may optionally include a single string for the name of the chemical entity. To capture chemical relationships, you may provide a single direct link to a specific chemical entity to indicate what it is a conjugate base of, which connects the base form or its different charge states to an acid, as well as a single direct link to a chemical entity to indicate what it is a conjugate acid of. For hierarchical organization, the class can be categorized under other molecule grouping classes using direct links to establish what it is a subtype of, allowing for arbitrarily deep classification hierarchies. Finally, the record may contain a single internal nested record to define a broader subclass categorization or metaclass relationship.

label: chemrof:AminoAcidSequenceInterval
description: When defining an Amino Acid Sequence Interval, the data model is strict and no outside properties are allowed, except for the explicitly ignored system type. Every record must specify exactly one string identifier. Beyond this required identifier, several optional attributes may be provided, each limited to a maximum of one entry unless otherwise noted. You may include a string for the chemical entity's name, a formal IUPAC name, and a general type designation. To describe the molecular structure, you can provide an InChI string alongside specific InChI sublayers: a main chemical formula sublayer that must consist entirely of uppercase letters, numbers, and periods, and an atom connections sublayer that details non-hydrogen bonds and must strictly begin with a lowercase 'c'. The model also accommodates SMILES strings to encode the molecular graph without chiral or isotopic details, as well as an empirical formula to show the relative proportion of atoms. Additionally, you may specify a sequence representation and a boolean flag to indicate whether the structure is a radical. For hierarchical relationships, the interval can be categorized under a broader parent class to denote specific groupings or species, which must be formatted as an internal nested record. Finally, a specific chemical role can be assigned to the interval, which must also be structured as an internal nested record.

label: chemrof:AtomGroupingByProperty
description: When curating an atom grouping by property, such as a metal atom, the data model is strictly closed, meaning no outside properties are permitted other than the explicitly ignored system type. Each record must specify exactly one string-based identifier. The grouping can also have at most one string-based name representing the chemical entity, such as nickel or carbon-16. To establish hierarchical categorizations, the record may include up to one OWL subclass relationship, formatted as an internal nested record, which connects specific elements or groupings to broader metaclasses. Finally, the grouping may contain direct links to molecule grouping classes to define subtype relationships, indicating that the current entity is a more specialized form of another grouping class.

label: chemrof:AtomGroupingClass
description: When defining an atom grouping class, which is used to classify atoms such as metal atoms, the data model is strictly closed, meaning no outside properties are permitted except for the standard system type. Every record must specify exactly one unique identifier as a text string. The record may also optionally include a single name for the chemical entity, such as nickel or carbon-16. To establish hierarchical relationships, the class can include at most one subclass reference, formatted as an internal nested record, which links it to a broader metaclass. Furthermore, the record may include direct links to molecule grouping classes to define a subtype relationship, indicating that this specific atom grouping is a more specialized form of a broader parent grouping class.

label: chemrof:AtomGroupingByPeriodicTablePlacement
description: When curating an atom grouping by periodic table placement, the data model is open, meaning additional properties beyond the core requirements are permitted. Every record must specify exactly one string-based identifier. The grouping can also provide at most one string-based name to describe the chemical entity, such as nickel or carbon-16. To establish hierarchical categorizations, the record may include up to one OWL subclass relationship, formatted as an internal nested record, which connects the entity to a broader class or metaclass. Furthermore, the grouping may define its specialization using the subtype of relationship. If provided, this must be a direct link to a specific molecule grouping class, indicating that the current entity is a more specialized form of that broader grouping.

label: chemrof:ChemicalInformation
description: When curating a chemical information record, the data model is designed to be flexible and open. This means that the structure is not strictly locked down, allowing curators to include additional relevant properties outside of the explicitly defined schema to capture comprehensive chemical data. Furthermore, standard system type declarations are safely ignored during validation, ensuring that the inclusion of basic structural metadata does not interfere with the extensible nature of the chemical information profile.

label: chemrof:ChargeState
description: When curating a Charge State, which applies to entities that may be positive, negative, or neutral, the data model remains open to accommodate additional properties beyond the core requirements. Within this record, you are permitted to specify at most one elemental charge. If provided, this value must be formatted as an integer to accurately reflect the net charge, calculated as the total number of protons minus the number of electrons.

label: chemrof:CationState
description: When curating a cation state, which serves as a mixin applied to any positively charged atom or molecule, the data model is flexible and allows for the inclusion of additional properties beyond the core definition. Within this record, you may specify at most one elemental charge to quantify the ion. If this elemental charge is provided, it must be expressed as a whole number of 1 or greater, accurately reflecting the number of protons minus the number of electrons in the entity.

label: chemrof:Charged
description: When curating a charged entity, which applies to any chemical structure carrying either a positive or negative charge, the data model is flexible and allows for the inclusion of supplementary properties beyond the core requirements. Within this structure, you may specify at most one elemental charge. If this property is included, it must be recorded as a whole number integer that represents the net difference calculated by subtracting the number of electrons from the number of protons.

label: chemrof:ChemicalGroupingByCharge
description: When curating a chemical grouping by charge, which serves as a grouping class that refines a charge-neutral entity, the data model is strictly closed, meaning no outside properties are permitted other than explicitly ignored system types. Each record must specify exactly one identifier as a text string. It may also include up to one name for the chemical entity, such as nickel or carbon-16, formatted as a string. To arrange these grouping classes into arbitrarily deep hierarchies, the record can include subtype relationships, which must be direct links or specific identifiers pointing to a molecule grouping class, ensuring this connection only occurs between two grouping classes. Finally, the entity may have at most one subclass relationship, represented as an internal nested record or unnamed structure categorized under an OWL class, which is used to establish hierarchical relationships where metaclasses represent groupings or specific countable elements.

label: chemrof:ChemicalMixture
description: When curating a chemical mixture, defined as a polyatomic entity consisting of two or more weakly bound parts with known or unknown stoichiometry, the data model enforces specific rules while remaining open to additional properties. Every chemical mixture must specify exactly one string-based identifier. It may include at most one standard string-based name, such as nickel or carbon-16, as well as a single string representing its IUPAC name. The mixture can be categorized under at most one general type and may define a single hierarchical relationship to an ontology class, formatted as an internal nested record, to indicate whether it is a subtype of a broader grouping or classified by a specific element. Additionally, it can specify at most one chemical role, which must also be structured as an internal nested record. To describe its composition, the record allows for a single empirical formula where numbers denote the relative proportion of atoms, such as CH2O for glucose. It may include SMILES strings, which provide an encoding of the molecular graph without chiral or isotopic information. For standardized representation, the mixture can have at most one overall InChI string. This can be further detailed by a single InChI chemical sublayer representing the main chemical formula, which must strictly consist of uppercase letters, numbers, and periods. It may also feature at most one InChI atom connections sublayer to describe how non-hydrogen atoms are bonded in sequence, which must always begin with the lowercase letter c. Finally, a single boolean value can be provided to indicate whether the chemical mixture is a radical.

label: chemrof:ChemicalSaltByAnion
description: When curating a chemical salt by anion, which serves as a grouping class for chemical salts named by their anionic constituent, the data model is strictly closed, meaning no outside properties are permitted other than standard system types. Every record must specify exactly one string-based identifier. Optionally, the record may include a single text-based name for the chemical entity, alongside at most one anionic component and at most one cationic component, both recorded as strings. To capture hierarchical relationships, the salt can be categorized under at most one overarching class, formatted as an internal nested record, which dictates how this entity relates to broader metaclass groupings or specific countable species. Additionally, the salt can be designated as a subtype of broader molecule grouping classes using a direct link to establish arbitrarily deep hierarchies, provided this connection strictly links two grouping classes together.

label: chemrof:ChemicalGroup
description: When defining a Chemical Group, the data model is strictly closed, meaning no outside properties are permitted other than standard system types. Every record must specify exactly one string-based identifier. You may provide at most one string-based name for the chemical entity, such as nickel or carbon-16, as well as at most one string-based IUPAC name. To establish hierarchical relationships, the group can be categorized under at most one OWL subclass, formatted as an internal nested record, which connects grouping classes like alkane to hydrocarbon or classifies specific elements. Additionally, you can specify at most one type and assign at most one chemical role, which must also be structured as an unnamed internal nested record. The record allows for SMILES strings, which are string encodings of the molecular graph lacking chiral or isotopic information; multiple valid SMILES can be provided since structures like ethanol can be written as CCO, OCC, or C(O)C. You may also include at most one InChI string. For more granular InChI details, you can provide at most one string for the InChI chemical sublayer representing the main chemical formula, which must consist entirely of uppercase letters, numbers, and periods. Similarly, you may include at most one string for the InChI atom connections sublayer, which describes how non-hydrogen atoms are connected by bonds and must begin with the lowercase letter c. The group can feature at most one empirical formula, where numbers denote the relative proportion of atoms, such as CH2O for glucose. Finally, you may indicate whether the group is a radical using at most one boolean value.

label: chemrof:ChemicalSalt
description: When defining a Chemical Salt, which is a chemical compound consisting of an ionic assembly of cations and anions, the data model is strictly closed, meaning no outside properties are permitted other than the explicitly ignored system type. Every record must specify exactly one string identifier. In addition, the salt may include a single string for the name of the chemical entity and at most one string for its formal IUPAC name. To represent its structure, the record can feature at most one empirical formula denoting the relative proportion of atoms, a single InChI string, and any number of SMILES strings that encode the molecular graph without chiral or isotopic information. It may also contain specific InChI components: at most one main chemical formula sublayer that must consist exclusively of uppercase letters, numbers, and periods, and a single atom connections sublayer string detailing non-hydrogen bond connections, which must strictly begin with a lowercase letter c. A single boolean value may be provided to indicate whether the salt is a radical, along with at most one general type designation. Furthermore, the salt can specify at most one chemical role, formatted as an internal nested record. Finally, it may be hierarchically categorized under at most one broader grouping or metaclass, also represented as an internal nested record, to define its subclass relationship within the ontology.

label: chemrof:ChemicalSaltByCation
description: When defining a chemical salt by cation, which serves as a grouping class for chemical salts named by their cation, the data model is strictly closed, meaning no outside properties are permitted other than explicitly ignored system types. Every valid record must specify exactly one string-based identifier. You may also provide a single name for the chemical entity, as well as at most one string detailing its cationic component and at most one string detailing its anionic component. To establish hierarchical categorizations, the record can include up to one internal nested record that categorizes it under a broader class, acting as a metaclass to represent groupings or specific countable species. Furthermore, the entity can be categorized under arbitrarily deep hierarchies by providing direct links to broader molecule grouping classes, ensuring that these subtype relationships specifically connect two grouping classes.

label: chemrof:ChemicalSaltGroupingClass
description: When defining a Chemical Salt Grouping Class, which serves to categorize chemical salts, the data model is strict and no outside properties are allowed other than explicitly ignored system types. Each record must specify exactly one string-based identifier and may include a single optional string for the name of the chemical entity. To describe its chemical composition, the class can optionally specify at most one string representing its cationic component and at most one string for its anionic component. For hierarchical organization, the record may include direct links to specific identifiers of Molecule Grouping Classes to denote what it is a subtype of, allowing these grouping classes to be arranged in arbitrarily deep hierarchies. Finally, the model permits at most one internal nested record to define an OWL subclass relationship, which categorizes the salt grouping within broader metaclasses.

label: chemrof:ChemicalRelationship
description: When defining a chemical relationship, which serves as a reified relationship within the system, the data model is open and permits the inclusion of outside properties. Within this structure, you may provide at most one subclass relationship, which must be formatted as an internal nested record that references an ontology class. This subclass property establishes hierarchical categorizations between a child entity and its parent class. Because most classes in this model act as metaclasses representing either broad chemical groupings or specific countable species, this hierarchical link conceptually supports two distinct curation cases. It can represent a subtype connection that links two grouping classes together, such as categorizing an alkane as a hydrocarbon, or it can represent a classification connection that assigns a specific chemical entity to a broader grouping, such as defining methane as a hydrocarbon or iron as a metal.

label: chemrof:Connectivity
description: When defining a Connectivity record, which acts as an abstract grouping to relate chemical entities through specific connectivity relationships, the data model remains flexible. Because this structure is not strictly closed, curators are permitted to include additional descriptive properties beyond the primary specifications. Furthermore, standard system type declarations are explicitly ignored during validation, allowing the focus to remain on the chemical connectivity data itself.

label: chemrof:Collection
description: When defining a Collection, the data structure is strictly closed, meaning no outside properties are allowed except for standard system classifications. Within this strict model, the collection can specify its constituent entities. Any provided entities must be formatted as specific identifiers or direct links that point exclusively to valid Domain Entities.

label: chemrof:ChiralityCenter
description: When curating a chirality center, which represents a stereocenter consisting of an atom holding a set of ligands in a spatial arrangement that is not superimposable on its mirror image, the data model is strictly controlled. To maintain data integrity, this record is completely closed, meaning no outside or unapproved properties are allowed to be added to the description, with the only exception being standard system type declarations.

label: chemrof:EntityWithAtomsEnumerated
description: When curating an entity with atoms enumerated, which serves as a structural mixin for any chemical entity where the constituent atoms and their exact occurrence counts are tracked—similar to the chemical layer in an InChI representation—the data model remains open, allowing for additional descriptive properties to be included alongside the standard rules. Within this structure, you may specify a single optional InChI chemical sublayer to denote the main chemical formula. If this formula is provided, it must be recorded as a text string that strictly follows a specific pattern, containing exclusively uppercase letters, numeric digits, and periods.

label: chemrof:EntityWithChargeSpecified
description: When defining an entity with a specified charge, which acts as a mixin for chemical records where the charge is explicitly detailed to correspond with the InChI charge layer, the data schema remains flexible and allows for additional descriptive properties. Within this structure, you are permitted to include a maximum of one InChI charge sublayer. If this sublayer information is provided, it must be recorded as a standard text string and is strictly required to begin with the letter q.

label: chemrof:EntityWithIsotopeSpecified
description: When curating an entity with an isotope specified, which serves as a mixin for chemical records where the isotopic composition is explicitly defined, the data model is flexible and permits the inclusion of additional unlisted properties. Within this structure, you may provide at most one InChI isotopic layer. If this layer is included, it must be recorded as a standard text string and is strictly required to begin with either the letter 'i' or 'h' to properly reflect the standard InChI isotopic notation.

label: chemrof:EntityWithConnectivitySpecificied
description: When curating an entity with connectivity specified, which serves as a mixin for chemical entities containing two or more atom occurrences with explicitly defined connectivity corresponding to the InChI /c layer, the data model remains open to additional properties. Within this structure, you may include at most one InChI atom connections sublayer. This sublayer must be formatted as a text string starting with the letter 'c', and it is used to describe the bond connections between sequentially numbered non-hydrogen atoms in the chemical formula. Furthermore, you may provide up to one InChI hydrogen connections sublayer. This must also be a text string, strictly beginning with the letter 'h', which details the exact number of hydrogen atoms connected to each of the previously defined heavy atoms.

label: chemrof:EntityWithStereochemistrySpecified
description: When curating an entity with stereochemistry specified, which serves as a mixin for chemical records where stereochemical details are explicitly declared corresponding to the /t, /m, and /s layers in an InChI string, the data model is open and permits the inclusion of additional unlisted properties. Within this structure, curators may optionally define three specific text-based sublayers, each limited to a maximum of one entry per record. If an InChI stereochemical double bond sublayer is specified, the text string must strictly begin with the letter 'b'. If an InChI tetrahedral stereochemical sublayer is included, the string must start with either 't' or 'm'. Finally, any provided InChI stereochemical type sublayer must be formatted as a text string that begins with the letter 's'.

label: chemrof:FullyCovalentlyBonded
description: When classifying a chemical entity as fully covalently bonded, the core structural requirement is that every pair of atoms within the entity must be transitively connected to one another through covalent bonds. This specific data model is intentionally flexible and open, meaning that curators are permitted to attach additional descriptive properties or annotations to the record beyond the base requirements, and standard system classifications are naturally accommodated during validation.

label: chemrof:GeneralizedMolecularStructureClass
description: When curating a generalized molecular structure class, which serves as a molecule grouping class defined by a chemical formula using variables, arithmetic expressions, and optional constraints such as using CnH2n+2 to group alkanes like methane, the data model is strictly closed, meaning no unlisted properties are permitted other than the standard system type. Every record must specify exactly one unique identifier as a text string. You may optionally provide a single name for the chemical entity, formatted as a text string. To establish hierarchical relationships, the record may contain at most one internal nested record defining an OWL subclass relationship, which categorizes the entity under a broader metaclass. Furthermore, to build arbitrarily deep classification hierarchies, the entity can be categorized under broader groups using direct links to other molecule grouping classes via the subtype relationship, provided this connection strictly links two grouping classes together.

label: chemrof:GeneralAcidBaseGroupingClass
description: When defining a general acid-base grouping class, which serves as a physiological molecule category that groups an acid together with all its conjugate bases, the data model must be strictly closed, allowing no outside properties other than the explicitly ignored system type. Every record must specify exactly one string-based identifier and may include at most one string-based name to label the chemical entity. To map chemical transformations, the grouping may provide a direct link to at most one chemical entity acting as its conjugate base, which connects the base form or its various charge states to the acid, alongside a direct link to at most one chemical entity representing its conjugate acid. To establish hierarchical relationships, the record may contain at most one unnamed internal nested record to define its subclass categorization under a broader metaclass. Furthermore, it may provide direct links to broader molecule grouping classes to indicate what it is a subtype of, enabling these categories to be arranged in arbitrarily deep taxonomic hierarchies.

label: chemrof:FunctionalGroup
description: When defining a functional group, the data model is strictly closed, meaning no outside properties are permitted other than standard system types. Every record must specify exactly one string identifier. You may optionally provide a single string for the general name of the chemical entity, a single string for its formal IUPAC name, and at most one general type designation. A single boolean value can be used to indicate whether the entity is a radical. Its function can be detailed using at most one internal nested record that defines its specific chemical role. Structural information can be captured using at most one standard InChI string, which can be supplemented by a single InChI chemical sublayer representing the main chemical formula; this sublayer must be formatted strictly using only uppercase letters, numbers, and periods. You may also include a single InChI atom connections sublayer to describe how non-hydrogen atoms are bonded to one another, and this string must always begin with a lowercase 'c'. The record allows for SMILES strings to encode the molecular graph without chiral or isotopic information, as well as at most one empirical formula to denote the relative proportion of atoms. Finally, the functional group may be categorized under a broader hierarchical class or grouping using at most one internal nested record.

label: chemrof:InchiObject
description: When curating an InChI object, which serves as a structured representation of an InChI string broken down into its distinct sublayers, the data model is designed to be open and flexible. Unlike strictly closed records, this schema permits the inclusion of supplementary properties beyond the core definitions, while standard system type classifications are inherently ignored by the validation rules.

label: chemrof:Location
description: When defining a location, which serves as a foundational grouping for entities representing either the abstract or concrete whereabouts of chemical substances or their specific parts, the data model is intentionally flexible. Unlike strict records, this classification is open, meaning curators are permitted to include additional descriptive properties beyond the core definition to fully capture the location's details, while standard system type declarations are naturally accommodated.

label: chemrof:GroupingClassForAcidsOrBases
description: When defining a grouping class for acids or bases, the record must specify exactly one string-based identifier. Beyond this required ID, the entry may optionally include a single string to specify the name of the chemical entity, such as nickel or carbon-16. To describe chemical relationships, the class can provide up to one direct link to a chemical entity representing its conjugate base, which connects the base form or its various charge states to the acid, as well as a maximum of one direct link to its conjugate acid. For hierarchical organization, the entity may contain up to one internal nested record to establish a formal subclass categorization under a broader parent class. Furthermore, to support arbitrarily deep classification hierarchies, the record can include direct links to broader molecule grouping classes to explicitly define what it is a subtype of. While these specific properties are regulated, the overall data model is open, meaning additional descriptive properties are permitted alongside these defined rules.

label: chemrof:IsomeraseReaction
description: When curating an isomerase reaction, defined as a reaction that converts a molecule from one isomer to another, the data model enforces a strict, closed schema where no unlisted properties are permitted other than standard system types. Every record must specify exactly one string-based identifier. Beyond this mandatory requirement, several optional attributes may be included, each limited to a maximum of one entry. These include a string representing the name of the chemical entity, such as nickel or carbon-16, a Proposed Reactions InChI representation, and a reaction SMILES representation. Additionally, the record may capture kinetic data by specifying a single reaction rate and a single reaction rate coefficient, both of which must be recorded as floating-point numbers. Finally, the reaction record may include a single type designation.

label: chemrof:ImpreciseChemicalMixture
description: When defining an imprecise chemical mixture—a macroscopic polyatomic entity composed of multiple chemical ingredients without specified stoichiometry or defined spatial arrangement, such as coal, tea tree oil, or heterogeneous metals—the data model is strictly closed, meaning no outside properties are permitted other than standard system types. Every record must specify exactly one string identifier. Beyond this, the model accommodates several optional attributes, allowing at most a single string for the entity's common name, a single string for its IUPAC name, and a single general type designation. To represent the chemical structure, you may provide a single empirical formula denoting the relative proportions of its atoms, as well as SMILES strings that encode the molecular graph without chiral or isotopic details. For InChI-based representations, the record permits a single standard InChI string, a single proposed Mixtures InChI representation, and a single MixFile location. It also supports specific InChI sublayers: a single main chemical sublayer string that must consist exclusively of uppercase letters, numbers, and periods, and a single atom connections sublayer string detailing non-hydrogen bond connections, which must begin with the lowercase letter c. The mixture may be flagged by a single boolean value indicating whether it is a radical. Finally, the model supports complex relationships through internal nested records, allowing you to define the proportional parts that make up the complex mixture, assign a single internal nested record for its chemical role, and provide a single internal nested record to categorize the mixture under a broader hierarchical metaclass or grouping.

label: chemrof:Material
description: When curating a Material, defined as a substance composed of repeated chemical units that has a specific arrangement of atoms or molecules, the data model must be strictly closed, permitting no outside properties other than the standard system type. Every material record must specify exactly one string-based identifier. In addition to this required identifier, the record may optionally include up to one string name, such as nickel or carbon-16, and at most one specific type designation. Finally, the material may feature a maximum of one repeat unit, which must be represented as a direct link to a recognized chemical entity.

label: chemrof:Mineral
description: When defining a mineral, the data model is strictly closed, meaning no outside properties are allowed except for standard system types. Each mineral record must specify exactly one string to serve as its unique identifier. You may optionally provide a single string for the name of the chemical entity, such as nickel or carbon-16. Additionally, the record can include at most one direct link to a specific chemical entity that represents its repeat unit. Finally, you are permitted to provide at most one optional designation for the mineral's type.

label: chemrof:MolecularDerivativeGroupingClass
description: When curating a molecular derivative grouping class, which serves to categorize derivatives of other molecules or molecular grouping classes, you must adhere to a strict data model where no outside properties are permitted aside from basic system types. Every entry must specify exactly one string-based identifier. You may optionally provide a single string to denote the name of the chemical entity. To trace its chemical lineage, the record can include at most one direct link to the specific chemical entity it is a derivative of. For hierarchical organization, you can establish what this class is a subtype of by providing direct links to parent molecule grouping classes; this allows for arbitrarily deep hierarchies, provided these links exclusively connect grouping classes. Finally, the entry may feature at most one internal nested record detailing its overarching ontological subclass relationship, a structural mechanism used to clarify whether the metaclass functions as a broad grouping or represents specific countable chemical species.

label: chemrof:Moiety
description: When defining a moiety, which represents a named part of a molecule that may itself be composed of smaller functional groups, the data model is strictly closed, meaning no outside properties are permitted other than standard system types. Each moiety must specify exactly one string identifier. Additionally, the record may include at most one string for its general chemical name, at most one formal IUPAC name, and a single type designation. To describe its chemical nature, the moiety can provide at most one boolean flag indicating whether it is a radical, as well as an optional internal nested record to define a single chemical role. The moiety may also be categorized under a broader grouping or hierarchical class using at most one internal nested record. For structural representation, the curation allows at most one empirical formula denoting the relative proportion of its atoms, alongside any number of SMILES strings used to encode the molecular graph without chiral or isotopic information. If specifying InChI details, the record permits at most one general InChI identifier. It can also include at most one InChI chemical sublayer string representing the main chemical formula, which must strictly consist of uppercase letters, numbers, and periods. Finally, the record may contain at most one InChI atom connections sublayer string to describe non-hydrogen bond connectivity, provided this specific string begins with a lowercase 'c'.

label: chemrof:MolecularComponent
description: When defining a molecular component, which represents a proper part of a molecule larger than a single atom, the data model is strictly closed, meaning no outside properties are permitted other than standard system types. Every record must specify exactly one unique identifier as a string. You may provide at most one standard name for the chemical entity, such as nickel or carbon-16, along with a single IUPAC name. To describe the structure, the component can include up to one empirical formula denoting the relative proportion of atoms, like CH2O for glucose, and a single overall InChI string. If breaking down the InChI, you may provide at most one InChI chemical sublayer for the main chemical formula, which must consist exclusively of uppercase letters, numbers, and periods. You can also include a single InChI atom connections sublayer detailing how non-hydrogen atoms are bonded, provided this string begins with a lowercase 'c'. The record allows for SMILES strings, which are text encodings of the molecular graph without chiral or isotopic information, noting that multiple valid strings like CCO or OCC can represent the same structure. Additionally, you can specify a single boolean value indicating whether the component is a radical, and at most one general type designation. If assigning a chemical role, it must be provided as a single internal nested record. Finally, the component may be categorized under at most one parent class using an internal nested record to define its hierarchical relationship, which connects specific elements to broader grouping metaclasses, such as classifying serine as an amino acid or methane as a hydrocarbon.

label: chemrof:MolecularComplex
description: When defining a molecular complex—which represents a chemical complex composed of at least one weakly interacting molecule, such as a chromosome, double-stranded DNA, or a protein-containing macromolecular complex—the data model is strictly closed, meaning no outside properties are permitted other than explicitly ignored system types. Every molecular complex must specify exactly one string-based identifier. Beyond this required identifier, the record may include at most one string for its general name, a single IUPAC name string, and at most one general type designation. To describe its chemical nature, the complex can provide a single boolean flag indicating if it is a radical, as well as at most one empirical formula where numbers denote the relative proportion of atoms. For structural representation, the record may feature any number of SMILES strings, which encode the molecular graph without chiral or isotopic information, but it is limited to at most one standard InChI string. If detailing specific InChI components, it may include a single main InChI chemical sublayer string that must consist exclusively of uppercase letters, numbers, and periods, alongside at most one InChI atom connections sublayer string—describing non-hydrogen atom bonds—which must always begin with a lowercase 'c'. Finally, the complex may define its function and taxonomy using unnamed structures: it can include at most one internal nested record to specify its chemical role, and at most one internal nested record to establish its hierarchical categorization, linking the specific complex to broader grouping metaclasses.

label: chemrof:MoleculeGroupingClassDefinedByAdditionOfAGroup
description: When defining a molecule grouping class defined by addition of a group, which represents a chemical entity defined in terms of a derivative of a molecule and the addition of a specific group, the data model is strict and no outside properties are allowed except for the explicitly ignored system type. Each record must specify exactly one string identifier. You may optionally provide a single name for the chemical entity, such as nickel or carbon-16. To establish hierarchical categorizations, the record may include at most one broader classification, formatted as an internal nested record, which reflects how metaclasses represent groupings or specific countable elements. Finally, to arrange these grouping classes into arbitrarily deep hierarchies, you may provide direct links to parent molecule grouping classes to specify what this entity is a subtype of.

label: chemrof:MolecularSubsequence
description: When defining a molecular subsequence, the data model is strictly closed, meaning no outside properties are permitted other than standard system types. Every molecular subsequence must specify exactly one string identifier. Beyond this required identifier, the record may include an optional single string for the name of the chemical entity, an optional single string for its IUPAC name, an optional single boolean flag indicating whether the structure is a radical, and an optional single type designation. For structural representation, the model allows an optional single empirical formula denoting the relative proportion of atoms, as well as any number of SMILES strings that encode the molecular graph without chiral or isotopic information. You may also include an optional single complete InChI string. If detailing specific InChI components, you can provide an optional single string for the InChI chemical sublayer representing the main chemical formula, which must consist exclusively of uppercase letters, numbers, and periods. Additionally, an optional single string can be provided for the InChI atom connections sublayer; this must begin with the lowercase letter 'c' and describes how non-hydrogen atoms are sequentially numbered and connected by bonds. Finally, the structure supports complex relationships through internal nested records: an optional single unnamed structure can be used to assign a specific chemical role, and another optional single internal nested record can establish hierarchical categorizations, linking the subsequence to broader grouping classes or metaclasses.

label: chemrof:MoleculeGroupingClassDefinedByComponents
description: When defining a molecule grouping class defined by components, which represents a grouping class defined as an exhaustive list of its components, curators must adhere to a strict data model that prohibits any outside properties other than the basic system type. Each record must specify exactly one string-based identifier. You may optionally provide a single string-based name for the chemical entity, such as nickel or carbon-16. The record can include at most one basic parthood relation, which must be formatted as an internal nested record linking to a chemical entity or grouping. To organize these grouping classes into arbitrarily deep hierarchies, the entity may contain direct links to broader molecule grouping classes to establish subtype relationships. Finally, the record allows for at most one hierarchical categorization, structured as an internal nested record, to define its subclass relationship within the broader metaclass system.

label: chemrof:Monomer
description: When defining a Monomer, the data model is strictly closed, meaning no outside properties are permitted other than standard system types. Every monomer must specify exactly one unique identifier as a string. To describe the entity, it may include at most one standard name, such as "nickel" or "carbon-16", as well as a single IUPAC name. The chemical composition can be represented by at most one empirical formula, which denotes the relative proportion of atoms, such as CH2O for glucose. For detailed structural representation, the monomer may include a single InChI string, along with specific InChI sublayers: an InChI chemical sublayer representing the main chemical formula, which must consist exclusively of uppercase letters, numbers, and periods, and an InChI atom connections sublayer that numbers non-hydrogen atoms and describes their bonds, which must always begin with a lowercase 'c'. The record can also feature SMILES strings, which provide a string encoding of the molecular graph without chiral or isotopic information, keeping in mind that multiple valid SMILES can represent the same structure. Additionally, a single boolean value can indicate whether the monomer is a radical. The monomer may specify at most one general type and a single chemical role, the latter of which must be structured as an internal nested record categorized under the Chemical Role class. Finally, the monomer can be hierarchically categorized under at most one grouping or metaclass using an internal unnamed structure to define its subclass relationship.

label: chemrof:Nanomaterial
description: When curating a nanomaterial, defined as a material where a single unit is sized between 1 and 100 nanometers in at least one dimension, the data model is strictly closed, meaning no unlisted properties are permitted aside from standard system types. Every valid record must specify exactly one string-based identifier. Additionally, the record may optionally include a single string representing the name of the chemical entity, such as nickel or carbon-16, as well as at most one general type classification. If the nanomaterial has a repeating structure, you may provide a maximum of one direct link to a specific identifier for the chemical entity that serves as its repeat unit.

label: chemrof:MonomericPolymericGroupingClass
description: When defining a Monomeric Polymeric Grouping Class, you must adhere to a strict data model where no outside properties are allowed, with the sole exception of the basic system type. Every record must specify exactly one string-based identifier. You may optionally provide a single string-based name for the chemical entity, such as nickel or carbon-16. The grouping class can specify at most one repeat unit, which must be provided as a direct link to a specific chemical entity. To establish hierarchical relationships, the record may include at most one subclass categorization formatted as an internal nested record pointing to a parent class, reflecting how metaclasses represent groupings or specific countable elements within the model. Furthermore, the entity can be categorized under broader molecule grouping classes using direct links to establish arbitrarily deep hierarchies, keeping in mind that this specific subtype relationship should only connect two grouping classes.

label: chemrof:DNASequenceInterval
description: When defining a DNA sequence interval, the data model is strictly closed, meaning no outside properties are permitted other than explicitly ignored system types. Every record must specify exactly one string-based identifier. You may optionally provide a single name for the chemical entity, such as nickel or carbon-16, as well as a single IUPAC name and a single type designation. The interval can include a single sequence representation, which by convention is always the DNA sequence, or the cDNA sequence in the case of RNA intervals. To describe its chemical makeup, the record allows for a single boolean flag indicating if it is a radical, and a single empirical formula where numbers denote the relative proportion of atoms, such as CH2O for glucose. For structural representations, you can provide a single InChI string along with its specific sublayers. The InChI chemical sublayer, representing the main chemical formula, is limited to a single string that must consist entirely of uppercase letters, numbers, and periods. The InChI atom connections sublayer, which describes how non-hydrogen atoms are bonded in sequence, must be a single string that strictly begins with a lowercase 'c'. Additionally, you may include SMILES strings to provide a molecular graph encoding without chiral or isotopic information, keeping in mind that multiple valid SMILES can represent the same structure. The interval can also specify a single chemical role, which must be formatted as an internal nested record. Finally, it may be categorized under a single hierarchical relationship, also structured as an internal nested record, to define its subclass. This categorization connects the entity to broader grouping classes or specific metaclasses, such as defining how a specific element is classified by a group or how one grouping is a subtype of another.

label: chemrof:PhysicochemicalEntity
description: When defining a physicochemical entity, which represents a discrete chemical structure, material structure, or subatomic particle type, the data model is flexible and allows for additional properties beyond the core requirements. Every record must specify exactly one unique identifier formatted as a text string. Curators may also optionally provide a single text string to serve as the name of the chemical entity, such as nickel or carbon-16. Additionally, the record can include at most one specific type classification to further categorize the entity.

label: chemrof:NucleotideSequenceInterval
description: When defining a nucleotide sequence interval, the data model is strictly closed, meaning no outside properties are permitted other than standard system types. Every record must specify exactly one string-based identifier. You may optionally provide a single string for the name of the chemical entity, as well as a single type designation. To capture the genetic information, you can include at most one sequence representation, which by convention should always be the DNA sequence, or the cDNA sequence if representing an RNA interval. The structure can be described using several optional chemical identifiers, including a single InChI string, a single string for the IUPAC name, and SMILES strings, which encode the molecular graph without chiral or isotopic information. If detailing specific InChI components, you may provide at most one InChI chemical sublayer to capture the main chemical formula, which must consist exclusively of uppercase letters, numbers, and periods. You can also include a single InChI atom connections sublayer to describe how non-hydrogen atoms are sequentially numbered and bonded; this string must always begin with the lowercase letter 'c'. Furthermore, the interval may feature at most one empirical formula to denote the relative proportion of atoms, and a single boolean flag to indicate whether the entity is a radical. For chemical and ontological classification, the interval can define at most one chemical role using an internal nested record. Finally, it may be hierarchically categorized under at most one parent class, also represented as an internal nested record, to establish its relationship to broader grouping classes or specific countable species.

label: chemrof:PolymerPart
description: When defining a polymer part, the data model is strictly closed, meaning no outside properties are permitted other than the standard ignored system type. Every polymer part must specify exactly one required string identifier. You may provide at most one standard name for the chemical entity, such as nickel or carbon-16, as well as a single IUPAC name. The chemical structure can be represented using various optional formats. This includes a single empirical formula, where numbers denote the relative proportion of atoms like CH2O for glucose, and at most one InChI string. If detailing the InChI further, you may include a single main chemical formula sublayer, which must consist exclusively of uppercase letters, numbers, and periods. Additionally, you can provide one InChI atom connections sublayer to describe which non-hydrogen atoms are connected by bonds; this specific string must begin with the lowercase letter c. The model also allows for SMILES strings, which encode the molecular graph without chiral or isotopic information, and because no maximum limit is set, you may provide multiple valid SMILES representations. The polymer part can be flagged with a single boolean value to indicate if it is a radical, and it may define at most one chemical role, which must be structured as an internal nested record. To establish hierarchical relationships, the polymer part can be categorized under at most one parent class using an internal unnamed structure. This categorization connects specific elements or grouping classes to broader metaclasses, such as linking methane to hydrocarbon or serine to amino-acid. Finally, a single general type classification may also be provided.

label: chemrof:PolyatomicEntity
description: When defining a polyatomic entity, which represents any chemical entity consisting of more than one atom, the data model is flexible and permits additional properties while ignoring standard system types. Every polyatomic entity must specify exactly one string-based identifier. You may provide at most one string for the entity's common name, such as nickel or carbon-16, as well as a single string for its formal IUPAC name. The entity can be assigned up to one type designation and at most one hierarchical categorization, formatted as an internal nested record, to classify it under a broader grouping or metaclass. It may also specify a single chemical role, structured as an internal nested record. To describe its chemical makeup, you can include at most one empirical formula that denotes the relative proportion of atoms, like CH2O for glucose, and at most one complete InChI string. The record allows for any number of SMILES strings to encode the molecular graph without chiral or isotopic information. If detailing specific InChI components, you may provide at most one InChI chemical sublayer string representing the main chemical formula, which must consist exclusively of uppercase letters, numbers, and periods. You can also include a single InChI atom connections sublayer string, which must begin with the lowercase letter 'c', to describe the specific bond connections between sequentially numbered non-hydrogen atoms. Finally, a single boolean value may be provided to indicate whether the entity is a radical.

label: chemrof:RNASequenceInterval
description: When defining an RNA sequence interval, the data model is strictly closed, meaning no outside properties are permitted other than standard system types. Every valid record must specify exactly one unique identifier string. To describe the entity, the record may include a single name string, up to one IUPAC name string, and a single boolean flag indicating whether the structure is a radical. It can also provide at most one sequence representation, which by convention should be the cDNA sequence for RNA intervals. For chemical and structural details, the interval allows for SMILES strings to encode the molecular graph without chiral or isotopic information, but is restricted to at most one standard InChI string. Furthermore, it may specify up to one InChI chemical sublayer string representing the main chemical formula—which must consist entirely of uppercase letters, numbers, and periods—as well as a single InChI atom connections sublayer string that must begin with the lowercase letter 'c' to describe how non-hydrogen atoms are bonded. The record may contain up to one empirical formula denoting the relative proportion of atoms, and a single general type designation. Finally, the interval may define at most one chemical role and can be categorized under at most one hierarchical relationship to connect the specific element to broader grouping classes; both of these complex attributes must be structured as internal nested records.

label: chemrof:Reaction
description: When curating a Reaction record, you must adhere to a strict data model that prohibits any unlisted properties, with the sole exception of the standard system type. Every reaction must specify exactly one string-based identifier. Beyond this required ID, the record may optionally include a single string representing the name of the chemical entity, such as nickel or carbon-16, as well as a single type designation. To capture the chemical transformation, you may provide at most one Proposed Reactions InChI representation and a single reaction SMILES representation. Furthermore, kinetic data can be recorded by optionally including a single floating-point value for the reaction rate and at most one floating-point value for the reaction rate coefficient.

label: chemrof:RacemicMixture
description: When defining a racemic mixture—a chemical compound containing equal amounts of left- and right-handed enantiomers of a chiral molecule, such as Thalidomide—the data model is strictly closed, meaning no outside properties are permitted other than standard system types. Each record must specify exactly one string identifier. Beyond this required identifier, the record may optionally include up to one string name for the chemical entity, a single type designation, and up to one string IUPAC name, which must strictly begin with the prefix "rac-". The molecular structure can be detailed using any number of SMILES strings to encode the molecular graph without chiral or isotopic information, up to one empirical formula denoting the relative proportion of atoms, and up to one standard InChI string. For more granular InChI data, the record allows up to one main chemical formula sublayer, which must consist entirely of uppercase letters, numbers, and periods, as well as up to one atom connections sublayer detailing non-hydrogen bonds, which must start with a lowercase "c". Finally, the mixture may include up to one boolean flag indicating if it is a radical, up to one chemical role, and up to one hierarchical categorization to group or classify the entity, with both the chemical role and the subclass categorization formatted as internal nested records.

label: chemrof:PreciseChemicalMixture
description: When defining a precise chemical mixture, which represents a polyatomic entity consisting of two or more weakly bound parts with a known stoichiometry, the data model is open and allows for additional properties beyond the core constraints while ignoring standard system types. Every record must specify exactly one required identifier as a text string. Optionally, it may include at most one standard name, such as nickel or carbon-16, and up to one IUPAC name, both formatted as text strings. The mixture can be hierarchically categorized under at most one parent class using an internal nested record to represent its grouping or specific classification, and it may also define up to one chemical role, similarly structured as an internal nested record. To describe its structure, the record may provide a single empirical formula denoting the relative proportion of atoms, a standard InChI string, and specific InChI sublayers. If provided, the InChI chemical sublayer representing the main chemical formula must be a string consisting exclusively of uppercase letters, numbers, and periods, while the InChI atom connections sublayer—detailing non-hydrogen atomic bonding sequences—must be a string that begins with a lowercase 'c'. The mixture may also include SMILES strings to encode the molecular graph without chiral or isotopic information, with no restriction on the number of entries. Finally, the record can specify at most one boolean value indicating whether the entity is a radical, and up to one general type designation.

label: chemrof:SequenceInterval
description: When defining a sequence interval, which represents a chain of connected monomers in a linear polymer such as a sub-region of an amino acid or nucleotide sequence, the data model is flexible and open, allowing additional properties beyond the standard constraints while ignoring standard system types. Every sequence interval must specify exactly one string-based identifier. You may optionally provide at most one string-based name for the chemical entity, such as nickel or carbon-16, as well as a single string-based IUPAC name. The interval can be flagged by at most one boolean value indicating if it is a radical, and it may define at most one chemical role, which must be provided as an internal nested record categorized under the Chemical Role class. For structural representation, you may include at most one sequence representation, a single standard InChI string, and at most one empirical formula, which denotes the relative proportion of atoms like CH2O for glucose. The model also permits SMILES strings, which encode the molecular graph without chiral or isotopic information. If detailing specific InChI components, you may provide at most one InChI chemical sublayer to represent the main chemical formula, which must be a string containing only uppercase letters, numbers, and periods. Additionally, you can include at most one InChI atom connections sublayer, which describes the bond connectivity between sequentially numbered non-hydrogen atoms; this must be a string that begins with a lowercase 'c'. Finally, the sequence interval may include at most one general type designation and at most one hierarchical subclass relationship, formatted as an internal nested record, to connect the specific element to a broader grouping or metaclass.

label: chemrof:SupramolecularPolymer
description: When defining a supramolecular polymer—described as a polymeric array of monomeric units connected by reversible and highly directional secondary interactions, namely non-covalent bonds—the data model is strictly closed, meaning no outside properties are permitted other than standard system types. Each record must specify exactly one string identifier. Additionally, the record may include at most one string for its general name, at most one formal IUPAC name string, and at most one general type designation. The chemical nature of the polymer can be further detailed with at most one boolean value indicating whether it is a radical, and at most one empirical formula denoting the relative proportion of its atoms. For structural representation, the record may contain at most one complete InChI string, alongside specific InChI components: at most one main chemical formula sublayer that must consist exclusively of uppercase letters, numbers, and periods, and at most one atom connections sublayer detailing non-hydrogen bond connections, which must strictly begin with the lowercase letter c. The model also permits SMILES strings to encode the molecular graph without chiral or isotopic information. To define its composition and function, the polymer may feature at most one direct link to a specific macromolecule it is a polymer of, and at most one direct link to a chemical entity representing its repeat unit. Finally, its function and classification can be captured using at most one internal nested record defining its chemical role, and at most one internal nested record hierarchically categorizing it under a broader grouping or metaclass.

label: chemrof:GroupingClass
description: When defining a Grouping Class, which represents a generic concept used to group more specific concepts or classify chemical entities, the data model is flexible and allows for additional unlisted properties. Every grouping class must specify exactly one string-based identifier. It may also include a single optional string-based name to clearly label the chemical entity, such as "nickel" or "carbon-16". To establish hierarchical relationships, the entity can be designated as a subtype of another grouping class to indicate it is a more specialized form; this relationship must be recorded as a direct link to a specific identifier. Additionally, the grouping class may define at most one overarching subclass categorization to connect it to a broader metaclass. Unlike the direct links used for subtypes, this specific subclass relationship must be formatted as an internal nested record, capturing the complex ontological mechanics of whether the entity functions as a broad grouping or a specific countable chemical species.

label: chemrof:ChemicalGroupingClass
description: When curating a chemical grouping class, which represents generic chemical concepts used to classify more specific entities such as how an amino acid groups serine or an alkane groups methane, the data model is flexible and permits additional unlisted properties alongside the core rules. Each record must specify exactly one string-based identifier. You may optionally provide a single string-based name for the chemical entity, such as nickel or carbon-16. To establish hierarchical categorizations, the record can include at most one internal nested record defining its OWL subclass relationship, which dictates how this metaclass relates to broader groupings or specific countable elements. Additionally, to indicate that the current concept is a more specialized form of another grouping, you may provide direct links to the specific identifiers of broader chemical grouping classes using the subtype relationship.

label: chemrof:MolecularComponentGroupingClass
description: When defining a molecular component grouping class, which serves to classify categories of molecular components such as an inorganic anion group, the data model is strictly closed, meaning no outside properties are allowed other than the explicitly ignored system type. Every record must specify exactly one string-based identifier. Additionally, it may include up to one string-based name representing the chemical entity, such as nickel or carbon-16. To establish hierarchical relationships, the class can have at most one OWL subclass categorization, which must be formatted as an internal nested record. This categorization reflects that most classes act as metaclasses representing groupings or specific countable elements, distinguishing between subtype relationships that connect grouping classes and classification relationships that connect specific elements to a group. Finally, the grouping class may define subtype relationships to indicate it is a more specialized form of another molecular component grouping class, and any such relationship must be provided as a direct link to the parent class's specific identifier.

label: chemrof:DomainEntity
description: When defining a domain entity, which represents a discrete, specific named entity in the domain of chemistry, the data model is intentionally flexible, allowing for additional properties beyond the specified rules while ignoring standard system types. For its primary attributes, the entity may include at most one string-based identifier. You can also provide a maximum of one string-formatted name to clearly label the chemical entity, such as nickel or carbon-16. Finally, the record may specify up to one type classification.

label: chemrof:MaterialGroupingClass
description: When defining a material grouping class, which serves to classify various materials, the data model is strict and no outside properties are allowed except for explicitly ignored system types. Each record must specify exactly one string-based identifier. You may also provide a single optional string to represent the name of the chemical entity, such as nickel or carbon-16. To establish hierarchical relationships, the entity can be designated as a subtype of another material grouping class to indicate it is a more specialized form; this relationship must be provided as a direct link to the parent grouping class. Furthermore, the entity may be categorized under a broader metaclass using an OWL subclass relationship. If provided, this subclass categorization is limited to a single entry and must be structured as an internal nested record. This specific subclass relationship helps capture complex hierarchical categorizations within the model, distinguishing between subtyping broad grouping classes, like connecting alkanes to hydrocarbons, and classifying specific elements, such as defining iron as a metal.

label: chemrof:PhysicochemicalEntityGroupingClass
description: When defining a Physicochemical Entity Grouping Class, the data model is flexible, allowing for additional properties beyond the core constraints. Each record must specify exactly one string-based identifier. You may also provide a single optional string to serve as the name of the chemical entity, such as nickel or carbon-16. To establish hierarchical relationships, the entity may be designated as a subtype of another Physicochemical Entity Grouping Class; this must be provided as a direct link to indicate that the current entity is a more specialized form of the parent grouping. Furthermore, the record may contain at most one OWL subclass relationship, formatted as an internal nested record pointing to a broader class category. This subclass structure is utilized because most classes in this model function as metaclasses representing either broad groupings or specific countable species, allowing curators to properly map grouping-to-grouping connections, such as an alkane being a hydrocarbon, or specific element classifications, such as methane being a hydrocarbon.

label: chemrof:ReactionGroupingClass
description: When defining a reaction grouping class, which serves to categorize reaction types such as dehalogenase or the breaking of a covalent bond, the data model is strictly closed, meaning no outside properties are permitted except for the standard system type. Every record must specify exactly one string-based identifier and may optionally include a single string representing the name of the entity. To establish hierarchical relationships, the record can provide direct links to other reaction grouping classes, indicating that it is a more specialized subtype of those broader groupings. Finally, the class may contain up to one internal nested record to define its broader categorization, detailing how this specific grouping is classified under a higher-level ontological metaclass.

label: chemrof:SubatomicParticle
description: When curating a subatomic particle, defined as a chemical entity below the granularity of an atom, the data model is strictly closed, meaning no outside properties are allowed other than the explicitly ignored system type. Each record must specify exactly one identifier as a text string. Additionally, the record may optionally include a single name for the chemical entity, formatted as a text string, such as nickel or carbon-16. You may also provide at most one subatomic particle type, which must be selected from a predefined list of specific ChEBI identifiers. Finally, the structure allows for an optional single general type classification.

label: chemrof:MoleculeGroupingClass
description: When defining a molecule grouping class, which is used to classify molecules such as carbohydrates, monosaccharides, amino acids, or polyacrylamides, curators must adhere to a strict data model that permits no outside properties other than the explicitly ignored system type. Every record must specify exactly one string-based identifier and may include at most one string representing the name of the chemical entity. To establish its broader ontological categorization, the class may contain up to one internal nested record defining its subclass relationship to a parent metaclass. Furthermore, because these categories can be arranged in arbitrarily deep hierarchies, the record may provide direct links to other molecule grouping classes to explicitly establish what it is a subtype of.

label: chemrof:SubatomicParticleOccurrence
description: When defining a subatomic particle occurrence, the data model is strictly closed, meaning no outside properties are permitted beyond the explicitly defined rules, with the sole exception of standard system types. Every record must specify exactly one subatomic particle that it is an occurrence of, which must be provided as a direct link to a specific subatomic particle identifier. Additionally, the record may include at most one count, represented as an integer value. Finally, the occurrence can optionally be categorized under at most one overarching class, formatted as an internal nested record. This categorization establishes a hierarchical relationship where the class acts as a metaclass representing either broad groupings or specific countable species, utilizing specific sub-properties to distinguish whether it connects two grouping classes together, such as an alkane being a hydrocarbon, or classifies a specific element into a broader group, like iron being a metal.

label: chemrof:AtomOccurrence
description: When defining an Atom Occurrence, which represents a specific instance of an atom within the context of a particular molecule such as one of the oxygen atoms in nitrate, the data model is strict and no outside properties are allowed except for standard system types. Each record must specify exactly one name as a text string and provide exactly one direct link to a specific Atom identifier to indicate what it is an occurrence of. The model also permits several optional integer properties, each limited to a maximum of one entry: the valence, indicating the number of electrons the atom uses in bonding; the oxidation number, reflecting the charge remaining when all ligands are removed heterolytically; the formal charge, representing the charge left when ligands are removed homolytically; and the coordination number, which counts the total number of atoms bonded to the atom of interest. Finally, the occurrence may include at most one hierarchical categorization, formatted as an internal nested record, to classify the entity under a broader grouping or metaclass.

label: chemrof:ChemicalEntity
description: When defining a chemical entity, which represents a discrete structure of one or more atoms describable by a chemical formula, the data model is open, allowing additional properties beyond the specified rules while explicitly ignoring system type declarations. Each entity must specify exactly one string-based identifier. It may also include at most one string-based name, such as nickel or carbon-16, and at most one string-based IUPAC name. To describe its structure, you may provide at most one empirical formula denoting the relative proportion of atoms, like CH2O for glucose. The entity can also include at most one InChI string and at most one InChI chemical sublayer; this sublayer acts as the main chemical formula and must be a string consisting exclusively of uppercase letters, numbers, and periods. Additionally, the entity may feature one or more SMILES strings, which encode the molecular graph without chiral or isotopic information, acknowledging that multiple valid SMILES can represent the same structure, just as CCO, OCC, and C(O)C all specify ethanol. A single boolean value can be provided to indicate whether the entity is a radical. The entity can have at most one type designation and at most one chemical role, with the role structured as an internal nested record. Finally, the entity may be hierarchically categorized under at most one parent class, also formatted as an internal nested record. In this model, these parent classes often act as metaclasses representing groupings or specific countable elements, allowing the entity to be connected either as a subtype of a broader grouping, like an alkane being a hydrocarbon, or classified directly into a grouping, such as methane being a hydrocarbon.

label: chemrof:ChemicalSimilarity
description: When defining a Chemical Similarity, which captures a relationship indicating some degree of shared characteristics between two or more chemical entities, the data model is strict and no outside properties are allowed except for standard system types. Within this strict structure, the record may specify a maximum of one hierarchical categorization, which must be formatted as an internal nested record. This categorization defines how the similarity relationship is classified under a broader parent class. Because most classes in this model act as metaclasses representing either broad groupings or specific countable chemical species, this hierarchical link helps establish the exact nature of the relationship, such as whether it connects grouping classes, like an alkane being a subtype of a hydrocarbon, or connects a specific element to a broader group, like methane being classified as a hydrocarbon.
